walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get started by placing the game board in the center of the table. The board shows a beautiful map of Europe with cities connected by colored and gray railway routes. Make sure everyone can reach the board comfortably. You will want some extra table space around the edges for card displays and personal play areas.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Train Car Cards
walkthrough.text: Now let's shuffle the train car card deck. These are the colorful cards you will collect and spend to claim routes. After shuffling, deal five cards face up in a row beside the board. Place the rest of the deck face down next to the display as a draw pile. Leave space for a discard pile nearby.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Destination Tickets
walkthrough.text: Separate the destination ticket cards into two piles. The long route tickets have a blue background and connect distant cities for big points. The regular tickets have a standard background. Shuffle each pile separately and keep them face down near the board.

walkthrough.title: Place the European Express Card
walkthrough.text: Find the European Express bonus card and place it face up beside the board where everyone can see it. This card awards ten bonus points at the end of the game to the player with the longest continuous path of connected routes. It is a nice reward for players who build efficiently.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Color
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color. Take all forty-five train car pieces and three train station pieces in your chosen color. Also grab the matching wooden scoring marker. You will use the train cars to claim routes on the board and the stations as emergency backup connections later in the game.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Train Car Cards
walkthrough.text: Deal four train car cards face down to each player. Pick up your cards and take a look, but keep them secret from the other players. These are your starting hand and you will build on them throughout the game by drawing more cards on your turns.

walkthrough.title: Deal Destination Tickets
walkthrough.text: Deal three long route tickets and one regular ticket to each player. Look at all four tickets. You must keep at least one of the long route tickets and you must keep the regular ticket. You may keep more if you like. Return any tickets you do not want to the bottom of their respective decks. Keep your chosen tickets secret.

walkthrough.title: Place Scoring Markers and Begin
walkthrough.text: Everyone places their scoring marker on the start space of the scoring track around the edge of the board. The player who has traveled the most in Europe goes first, or just pick randomly. Play proceeds clockwise. You are all set to start building railways across Europe!

summary.title: Lay Out the Board
bullets: Place the Europe map board in the center of the table
Ensure all players can reach the board comfortably

bullets: Shuffle train car deck → deal 5 face up beside the board
Place remainder face down as draw pile

summary.title: Prepare the Destination Tickets
bullets: Separate long route (blue) and regular destination tickets
Shuffle each pile separately, keep face down

summary.title: Place the European Express Card
bullets: Place European Express card face up near the board
Awards 10 pts to the player with the longest continuous path

bullets: Take 45 train cars + 3 train stations + 1 scoring marker in your color

bullets: Deal 4 train car cards face down to each player

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: In Ticket to Ride: Europe, we are railway barons building train routes across the continent. Our goal is to score the most points by claiming routes between cities, completing destination tickets, and earning the longest route bonus. The game ends shortly after any player runs low on train cars.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: One Action Only
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must do exactly one of four possible actions. You can draw train car cards, claim a route, draw destination tickets, or build a train station. You cannot do more than one of these per turn. Choose the action that best fits your current situation and long-term plan.

walkthrough.title: Action One: Draw Train Car Cards
walkthrough.text: Draw two train car cards to add to your hand. You can take from the five face-up cards or draw blind from the top of the deck, in any combination. However, if you take a face-up locomotive card, that counts as both of your draws. Locomotives are wild and very powerful, so they cost your full drawing action.

walkthrough.title: Action Two: Claim a Route
walkthrough.text: To claim a route, play train car cards from your hand matching the color and length of the route. For example, a blue route that is four spaces long requires four blue cards. Place your train car pieces on the route and immediately score points based on the route length. Gray routes can be claimed with any single color of matching cards.

walkthrough.title: Locomotives Are Wild
walkthrough.text: Locomotive cards are wild and can substitute for any color when claiming a route. You can use as many locomotives as you need in a single claim. However, some routes specifically require locomotives, particularly ferry routes. We will cover those special routes in a moment.

walkthrough.title: Ferries and Tunnels
walkthrough.text: Ferry routes have locomotive symbols on some spaces. You must play actual locomotive cards for those spaces. The remaining spaces can be filled with matching color cards as normal. Tunnel routes are marked with a special outline and add risk to your claim attempt.

walkthrough.title: How Tunnels Work
walkthrough.text: When you attempt to claim a tunnel route, first play the required cards. Then reveal three cards from the top of the draw pile. For each revealed card that matches the color you played, or is a locomotive, you must play one additional matching card. If you cannot or choose not to pay the extra cost, your attempt fails and you take all your cards back.

walkthrough.title: Action Three: Draw Destination Tickets
walkthrough.text: Return unwanted tickets to the bottom of the deck. Completed tickets score their printed point value at the end of the game. However, any ticket you keep but fail to complete will subtract that many points from your score.

walkthrough.title: Action Four: Build a Train Station
walkthrough.text: You can build a train station on any city that does not already have one. Your first station costs one card of any color. Your second station costs two cards of the same color. Your third station costs three cards of the same color. A station lets you use one route belonging to another player into that city when checking destination ticket completion.

walkthrough.title: Double Routes and Player Count Rules
walkthrough.text: Some pairs of cities have two parallel routes between them. In games with four or five players, both routes may be claimed by different players. In games with two or three players, only one of the two parallel routes may be claimed. The same player can never claim both routes between the same pair of cities.

walkthrough.title: Route Scoring Table
walkthrough.text: Let's go over the points you earn immediately when claiming a route. A one-car route scores one point. Two cars score two points. Three cars score four points. Four cars score seven points. Five cars score ten points. Six cars score fifteen points. An eight-car route scores twenty-one points. Longer routes give disproportionately more points.

walkthrough.title: Face-Up Card Refresh Rule
walkthrough.text: Whenever a face-up card is taken, immediately replace it from the draw pile. If at any point three or more of the five face-up cards are locomotives, discard all five face-up cards and deal five new ones. This keeps the display from being clogged with expensive locomotive cards.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Trigger
walkthrough.text: The endgame begins when any player has two or fewer train car pieces remaining at the end of their turn. Every player, including the player who triggered the end, gets one final turn. After that final round, we move to scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Route points are already on the scoring track. Now add points for each completed destination ticket and subtract points for each failed ticket. Each unused train station earns four bonus points. Finally, the player with the longest continuous path of connected routes receives the ten-point European Express bonus. Highest total score wins, with ties broken by most completed destination tickets.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: Build train routes across Europe to score points
Complete destination tickets, claim routes, earn longest route bonus

walkthrough.title: Welcome Aboard
walkthrough.text: We are going to walk through the opening turns of a four-player game. Our players are Anna with the blue trains, Ben with the red trains, Chloe with the green trains, and Dave with the yellow trains. Follow along even if you have a different number of players at your table.

walkthrough.title: Examining Starting Tickets
walkthrough.text: Everyone has received their four train car cards and their destination tickets. Anna looks at her long route tickets and sees Paris to Moscow, Madrid to Zurich, and Copenhagen to Erzurum. She also has a regular ticket for Berlin to Rome. She decides to keep Paris to Moscow for the big points and Berlin to Rome because they overlap nicely. She returns the other two.

walkthrough.title: Planning Your Route Network
walkthrough.text: Anna studies the board and traces a path from Paris through Munich, Vienna, and Budapest toward Moscow. She notices her Berlin to Rome ticket can share the Munich connection. This is key strategy: overlapping tickets use the same routes to score multiple times. Let's watch each player take their first few turns.

walkthrough.title: Anna's First Turn: Drawing Cards
walkthrough.text: Anna needs blue cards for the Paris to Munich route. She looks at the five face-up cards and spots a blue card and a locomotive. She takes the blue card first, then draws blindly from the deck since taking the face-up locomotive would cost both draws. Smart move. She now has five cards in hand and is building toward her first route claim.

walkthrough.title: Ben's First Turn: Drawing Cards
walkthrough.text: He needs red and orange cards for routes in the Mediterranean. He takes two cards from the face-down deck, hoping for useful colors. He gets a red card and a white card. Not perfect, but the red card helps. Most early turns involve drawing cards to build up your hand before claiming routes.

walkthrough.title: Chloe's Turn: A Common Mistake
walkthrough.text: Chloe has three green cards in her hand and sees a three-car green route on the board between Marseille and Barcelona. She immediately claims it, placing her three green train cars on the route and scoring four points. It feels great to score early! But here is the problem: this route does not connect to any of her destination tickets.

walkthrough.title: Mistake One: Claiming Routes That Do Not Help Your Tickets
walkthrough.text: Chloe scored four points, but she spent three cards and three train cars on a route that does not advance any of her tickets. Those cards could have gone toward routes she actually needs. The lesson here is to always check whether a route connects to your destination ticket network before claiming it. Scoring points is good, but completing tickets is usually worth far more.

walkthrough.title: Dave's Turn: Grabbing a Key Route
walkthrough.text: Dave notices that the route from Budapest to Bucharest is a crucial connection for his tickets and it only has one path. He has the right cards already, so he claims it immediately. Claiming contested routes early, before opponents can block you, is an important part of the game. Dave scores points now and secures his path for later.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Anna Claims a Route
walkthrough.text: On her next turn, Anna has enough cards to claim the Paris to Munich route. She plays her cards, places her train cars, and scores points. Her route network is starting to take shape. She can see the path from Paris to Moscow forming one segment at a time. Patience in collecting cards pays off with efficient route claims.

walkthrough.title: Ben Spots a Locomotive Opportunity
walkthrough.text: Ben sees a face-up locomotive in the display. Locomotives are wild and extremely valuable, but taking one from the display costs your entire draw action. Ben weighs his options and decides the locomotive is worth it because he needs it for a ferry route across the Mediterranean. Sometimes one powerful card is better than two ordinary ones.

walkthrough.title: Chloe Draws New Destination Tickets
walkthrough.text: Chloe decides to draw destination tickets to find ones that connect to her existing Marseille to Barcelona route. She draws three tickets and finds one that includes Barcelona. She keeps it and returns the other two. Now that disconnected route is part of a ticket network. Drawing new tickets mid-game can salvage routes and add scoring opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Two: Forgetting About Tunnels
walkthrough.text: Dave attempts to claim a tunnel route from Zurich to Milan. He plays three green cards confidently. But wait, this is a tunnel! He reveals three cards from the draw pile and two of them are green. Dave must pay two additional green cards, but he only has one left. His attempt fails and he takes all his cards back, wasting his entire turn.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Budget Extra for Tunnels
walkthrough.text: Tunnel routes are risky because you might need to pay zero, one, two, or even three extra cards. The best approach is to have one or two extra cards in the matching color before attempting a tunnel. Locomotives help too since they always count as extra payment. If you cannot afford to fail, wait until you have a comfortable buffer of cards.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Three: Ignoring the Station Option
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Anna discovers that Ben has claimed the only route into Moscow from the west. Her Paris to Moscow ticket looks doomed. Anna panics and starts drawing cards for a long detour around the north. But she has completely forgotten about train stations. A station placed in Moscow would let her use Ben's route to complete her ticket.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Stations Are Your Safety Net
walkthrough.text: Train stations exist specifically for situations where an opponent blocks a critical connection. Placing a station lets you use one route belonging to another player into that city. Before planning a long and expensive detour, check whether a single station could solve your problem for just one card. Remember, unused stations are worth four points each, so only build them when you truly need them.

walkthrough.title: The Network Comes Together
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, Anna places a station in Moscow and completes her Paris to Moscow ticket. Ben finishes his Mediterranean routes using the locomotive he saved. Chloe connects her new ticket through Barcelona. Dave successfully claims the Zurich to Milan tunnel on his second attempt with extra cards in reserve. Everyone's route networks are taking shape beautifully.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have just walked through the key moments of a real Ticket to Ride: Europe game. You saw how to plan overlapping tickets, why claiming routes early matters, and how tunnels, ferries, and stations add strategic depth. Remember Chloe's lesson about claiming useful routes, Dave's lesson about budgeting for tunnels, and Anna's lesson about using stations as safety nets.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Ride
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your own game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy on ticket selection and route planning.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card types, route scoring, and special route rules.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. All aboard!

walkthrough.title: Pick Overlapping Tickets
walkthrough.text: The single best strategy in Ticket to Ride: Europe is choosing destination tickets that share common routes. When two tickets both need the Munich to Vienna connection, you build that route once and it counts toward completing both tickets. During initial ticket selection, look at the map and trace paths for each ticket. If two or three tickets share a corridor, keep all of them.

walkthrough.title: Claim Critical Routes Early
walkthrough.text: Some routes on the board are bottlenecks where many players' tickets converge. Routes through Budapest, Vienna, and Istanbul are highly contested. If a route is critical for your ticket network and has no alternate path, claim it as soon as you have the cards. Waiting too long risks an opponent taking it first, forcing you to use a station or take a costly detour.

walkthrough.title: Balance Drawing and Claiming
walkthrough.text: New players often fall into one of two traps: either claiming tiny routes immediately or hoarding cards for too long. The best approach is to draw cards until you can claim a meaningful route of three or more cars, then claim it. Claiming small one-car and two-car routes is fine when they complete a critical connection, but do not waste turns on short routes that do not advance your tickets.

walkthrough.title: Keep Your Plans Secret
walkthrough.text: Do not make your destination cities obvious by claiming routes in a straight line from start to finish. If opponents can guess your tickets, they might intentionally block your routes. Instead, claim routes from the middle of your network outward, or build from both ends toward the center. This makes it harder for others to predict and block your plans.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Long Routes for Points
walkthrough.text: The scoring curve heavily rewards long routes. A six-car route is worth fifteen points, while six one-car routes would only score six points total. Whenever possible, aim for routes of four cars or longer. They are harder to collect cards for, but the point payoff is enormous. The eight-car route is worth twenty-one points all by itself.

walkthrough.title: Use Stations Wisely
walkthrough.text: Train stations are your insurance policy against being blocked. Do not build them early in the game when you might find alternate routes. Wait until you actually need one to complete a ticket. Each unused station is worth four bonus points at game end, so building all three stations when you only needed one costs you eight points. Save them for when an opponent truly blocks a critical connection.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Train Count
walkthrough.text: Keep an eye on how many train cars each player has remaining. When someone gets close to triggering the endgame with two or fewer trains, you need to shift priorities. Make sure your most valuable tickets are completed before the final round. If you are the one running low, consider whether triggering the endgame helps you or gives opponents one more turn to finish their big tickets.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the biggest beginner mistakes all in one place. First, keeping too many destination tickets at the start and failing to complete them, resulting in heavy point deductions. Second, ignoring tunnels and attempting them without backup cards. Third, forgetting that stations exist when blocked. Fourth, drawing new tickets late in the game when there is no time to complete them. Fifth, building stations you did not need and losing twelve easy bonus points.

bullets: Choose tickets that share common route corridors
Build one route → counts toward completing multiple tickets

bullets: Bottleneck routes (Budapest, Vienna, Istanbul) get contested fast
Claim essential routes ASAP — waiting risks being blocked

bullets: Draw until you can claim a 3+ car route, then claim
Avoid wasting turns on small routes that don't advance tickets

summary.title: Keep Your Plans Secret
bullets: Don't build in a straight line from start to finish
Claim from the middle outward to disguise your destinations

summary.title: Prioritize Long Routes for Points
bullets: 6-car route = 15 pts vs six 1-car routes = 6 pts
8-car route = 21 pts — aim for 4+ car routes when possible

summary.title: Use Stations Wisely

Each unused station = 4 bonus pts at game end
Don't build early when alternate routes might still be available

summary.title: Watch the Train Count

Complete high-value tickets before the final round

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Keeping too many tickets → failing them loses massive points
Attempting tunnels without backup cards
Forgetting stations exist when blocked
Drawing new tickets too late to complete them

walkthrough.title: Ticket Stacking
walkthrough.text: Advanced players deliberately choose tickets that create a dense network through central Europe. The corridor from Paris through Munich, Vienna, Budapest, and onward to the east serves as a highway that completes multiple tickets simultaneously. When selecting tickets, look for three or more that share at least two cities. A stacked network lets you score eighty or more points from tickets alone while using fewer total routes.

walkthrough.title: Locomotive Economy
walkthrough.text: Locomotives are the most powerful cards in the game because they work as any color. Advanced players strategically collect locomotives from the deck rather than the display to avoid telegraphing their plans. Drawing from the deck gives you a roughly one in eight chance of a locomotive per draw. Stockpiling three or four locomotives lets you claim almost any route regardless of color, giving you maximum flexibility.

walkthrough.title: Blocking Opponent Routes
walkthrough.text: Observant players can read opponents' likely destinations by watching which routes they claim. If you notice someone building toward a bottleneck city, claiming the route they need can devastate their plans. This is most effective when the blocking route also helps your own network. Pure spite blocking wastes your resources, but opportunistic blocking that serves double duty is a hallmark of expert play.

walkthrough.title: Tunnel Risk Assessment
walkthrough.text: Experienced players calculate tunnel risk before committing. Each revealed card has roughly a one in eight chance of matching your color, plus a one in eight chance of being a locomotive. With three reveals, the average extra cost is about one card. Budget two extra cards for comfort, but recognize that sometimes the risk is worth taking with only one spare. If the tunnel completes a critical connection, attempting it with moderate reserves is better than spending three turns collecting perfect backup.

walkthrough.title: European Express Strategy
walkthrough.text: The ten-point longest route bonus can swing the outcome of close games. To compete for it, build your route network as a single continuous chain rather than separate branches. Each branch point splits your longest path. If you are naturally building a long east-west corridor for your tickets, you are already positioned for the bonus. Track your longest path mentally and compare it to opponents' visible networks.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Timing
walkthrough.text: Controlling when the game ends is a powerful strategic lever. If you have completed your tickets and your opponent clearly has not, depleting your trains quickly forces the endgame and traps them with failed tickets worth negative points. Conversely, if you need more time, avoid claiming routes and focus on drawing cards to delay. The player who controls the endgame timing often controls the final score difference.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Ticket Draws
walkthrough.text: Drawing additional destination tickets mid-game is a calculated risk that advanced players use when their network is already strong. If you have a well-connected chain across central Europe, new tickets that connect cities within your existing network are essentially free points. The danger is drawing tickets that require building in a completely new direction. Only draw mid-game tickets when you have a broad network and enough trains to handle a worst-case draw.

walkthrough.title: Station Denial and Late-Game Station Play
walkthrough.text: At the highest level, stations become both offensive and defensive tools. If you suspect an opponent needs a specific route, claiming it forces them to spend a station, which costs them four end-game bonus points. On defense, saving your stations until the final few turns gives you maximum information about which connections you actually need. A station played on the last turn to complete a twenty-point ticket is one of the most efficient plays in the game.

summary.title: Ticket Stacking
bullets: Choose 3+ tickets sharing common central Europe corridors
Dense network = 80+ ticket pts using fewer total routes

summary.title: Locomotive Economy
bullets: Draw from deck for ~1/8 chance of locomotive per draw
Stockpile 3-4 locomotives for maximum route flexibility
Avoid telegraphing plans by taking face-up locomotives

summary.title: Blocking Opponent Routes
bullets: Watch opponents' route patterns to read their destinations
Block bottleneck routes that also serve your own network
Pure spite blocking wastes resources — dual-purpose blocking wins

summary.title: Tunnel Risk Assessment
bullets: Average extra cost ≈ 1 card per tunnel attempt
Budget 2 extra cards for comfort, 1 for calculated risk
Critical connections worth attempting with moderate reserves

summary.title: European Express Strategy
bullets: Build network as 1 continuous chain, not separate branches
Branch points split your longest path — minimize them
Track your chain length vs. opponents' visible networks

summary.title: Endgame Timing
bullets: Deplete trains fast if your tickets are done and opponents' aren't
Slow down and draw cards if you need more time
Controlling endgame timing controls score difference

summary.title: Mid-Game Ticket Draws
bullets: Draw new tickets only when your network is broad
Tickets within existing chain = free points
Only risk it with enough trains to handle worst-case draws

summary.title: Station Denial and Late-Game Station Play
bullets: Claiming opponent's needed route → forces them to spend a station (-4 pts)
Save your stations until final turns for maximum information
Last-turn station completing a 20-pt ticket = huge efficiency

entries.definition: Colored cards used to claim routes. Eight colors plus locomotive (wild). Draw 2 per turn from face-up display or deck.
Wild card that substitutes for any color when claiming routes. Taking a face-up locomotive costs your entire draw action (both draws). Required for ferry route locomotive spaces.
Card showing two cities and a point value. Completed tickets add points; failed tickets subtract points. Must keep at least one when drawing.
Blue-backed destination ticket connecting distant cities for high point values. Each player receives three at setup and must keep at least one.
Colored plastic pieces (45 per player) placed on routes when claimed. Running low to 2 or fewer triggers the endgame.

Allows use of one opponent's route into that city for ticket completion. First costs 1 card, second costs 2 same-color cards, third costs 3 same-color cards. Unused stations worth 4 points each at game end.
Wooden piece tracking each player's score around the board edge. Route points are added immediately when routes are claimed.
Bonus card awarding 10 points to the player with the longest continuous path of connected routes at game end.
Play matching train car cards equal to the route's length and color, then place your train cars on the board and score points immediately.
Route with locomotive symbols on certain spaces. Those spaces require actual locomotive cards. Remaining spaces accept matching color cards.
Route with outlined borders. After playing cards, reveal 3 from the draw pile. Each matching color or locomotive revealed requires 1 additional card or the attempt fails.
Route with no specific color. Can be claimed using any single color of matching cards (all cards played must be the same color or locomotives).
Two parallel routes between the same pair of cities. In 2-3 player games, only one may be claimed. Same player can never claim both.
Points scored immediately when claiming: 1 car = 1 pt, 2 = 2, 3 = 4, 4 = 7, 5 = 10, 6 = 15, 8 = 21.
When any player has 2 or fewer train cars remaining after their turn, every player gets one final turn before the game ends.
Route points (tracked during game) plus completed ticket points minus failed ticket points plus unused station bonuses (4 each) plus European Express (10 points for longest continuous path).
Five train car cards dealt face up beside the board. Replaced immediately when taken. If 3+ are locomotives, discard all 5 and deal new ones.
An unbroken chain of a player's claimed routes connected city to city. Used to determine the European Express bonus. Branch points do not break the path but only the longest single chain counts.
